name: Sacred Rhythms, Living Histories of Jogappa community
description:
Long before the language of inclusion entered our workplaces, India held space for gender in all its complexity through diverse cultural and spiritual traditions. The Jogappas are one such living tradition — a transgender community whose identity, livelihood, and spiritual practice have remained deeply intertwined for centuries through devotion to Goddess Yellamma.

Sacred Rhythms, Living Histories of Jogappa community invites audiences into this world through an intimate cultural experience where music, storytelling, and handmade instruments carry forward a tradition rooted at the intersection of faith, folklore, and identity. Drawing from rich oral histories across north Karnataka, south Maharashtra, west Telangana, and Andhra Pradesh, the Jogappas perform devotional compositions in praise of Goddess Yellamma while sharing glimpses into their lived realities and cultural heritage.

In collaboration with the Solidarity Foundation, this interactive session brings together the lived cultural knowledge of the Jogappa tradition and academic perspectives on its history, music, and spiritual practice, offering audiences a rare opportunity to listen closely, engage meaningfully, and deepen their understanding of this powerful living art form.

name: Escaping our Existence
description:
The humans have vanished, and you’ve just landed on Earth. Join one of three expeditions and unravel the clues they left behind. Hidden in plain sight are clues about the paintings in Beneath the Turning Sky for you to solve and find your way out of the exhibition space!
Join us for this mystery-filled adventure!
This programme is hosted in conjunction with the Teen Takeover programme.

name: Wear the Sky
description:
“A hat is a mood. It’s a feeling,” says the famed milliner Philip Treacy. Headgear–designed, sculpted, pieced together–can be very expressive accessories. Join us for a workshop where we make our own funky headgear and write slam poetry inspired by the Beneath the Turning Sky exhibition. 

This is a workshop for you to explore how costume and voice can come together to aid self-expression in fun, exciting ways!

This programme is hosted in conjunction with the Teen Takeover programme.

name: Queer Film Evenings
description:
Join us for a weekend of queer short film screenings that bring into focus stories of identity, resistance, joy, and community from across the country. Through intimate and thought-provoking narratives, these films explore the realities of queer lives, reflecting on visibility, belonging, resilience, and self-expression in a time marked by increasing invisibilisation and protest.

Spanning diverse experiences and perspectives, the screenings invite audiences to engage with stories that challenge silence and reclaim space through the power of storytelling. Accompanied by conversations and reflections with speakers and community voices, the programme creates space for dialogue rooted in solidarity, and queer joy.

The film list will be announced shortly.

This programme is part of LGBTQIA+ programming and is an attempt to amplify narratives from within the queer community through film, dialogue, and collective reflection.

name: Paper Gardens: Art, Botany and Empire
description:
Join us for a guided walk of the exhibition Paper Gardens: Art, Botany, and Empire by Breanne Coelho from the MAP Team.
Between the 17th and 20th centuries, the Indian subcontinent was the site of extensive botanical surveys under colonial rule. Plant collection and classification were closely tied to imperial economic, medical, and political interests, and botanical illustration played a central role in this project. 
Bringing together a new and historically significant collection of botanical art from this period, the exhibition draws on recent scholarship to recover the often uncredited identities and histories of the Indian artists who made them.

name: Teens After-Hours
description:
Being a teenager isn't easy. You're navigating a whole new world, trying to make sense of it, figure out what excites you, what doesn't, and who you're becoming along the way. As part of our Teen Takeover programme, we're opening the doors for an after-hours session that's entirely yours! Come together with other teenagers to explore Beneath the Turning Sky, our permanent exhibition, before settling in for a peer-led art therapy session. This session will include meditative mandalas, colouring, drawing and more creative ways to slow down, tune in and express yourself.

No fixed agenda. No pressure. Just space to hang out, listen to each other and make something together inside the museum.

This programme is hosted in conjunction with the Teen Takeover programme.

name: Nature Journaling
description:
Nature journaling is a creative practice that encourages us to slow down, observe closely, and connect with the natural world through curiosity and reflection. In this session, participants will explore nature using simple tools like sketching, writing, and mindful observation. From leaves and flowers to insects, textures and patterns, the session invites participants to notice the small details often missed in everyday life. 

Inspired by the MAP's Living Botanicals, the programme encourages participants to think about the relationship between plants, observation, storytelling, while exploring how nature has been documented, collected, and represented over time.

name: Framing Living Spaces: Visual Storytelling Through Photography & AI
description:
Conceptualised and facilitated by Srivatsan Sankaran, this session encourages participants to engage with Living Botanicals through photographic observation and image-based storytelling. As participants move through the space, they will reflect on how movement, body language, and silent expression can be translated into compelling visual narratives, resonating deeply with sign language and Deaf visual culture.

The session also introduces perspectives on how photography and AI can interact with visual languages, opening new possibilities for documenting gestures, expressions, and moments of connection. Through the process of photographing and interpreting the space, participants will experiment with telling stories through images inspired by the environment and their own expressions.

By combining a guided photo walk with collaborative visual exploration, the workshop creates a space for participants to interpret gesture, communication, and space through their own visual narratives.

name: Make at MAP
description:
What does stitching mean to you? Is it an act of creation, a therapeutic process, or simply a fun activity? Either way, drop in for a relaxed stitching session where you layer your thoughts into the process and create a souvenir to take home.

Make @ MAP is a series that allows you to explore our collection in an engaging and hands-on manner. These sessions allow you to create and take home a special souvenir of your visit. It is perfect for all ages — so bring your friends and family and get ready to make some art! No experience is necessary, just a willingness to have fun and experiment with new materials.

name: Sketch with PencilJam
description:
View the museum with new eyes as you make your way through various exhibitions with a sketchbook in hand. You don’t need to be a professional to join, we’ll have a briefing at the start to guide you through this drawing session.

All you need is an open mind and:

-Sketchbook or A4 paper

-Pencil (wood or mechanical) and eraser

-Gel pens or fine liners for outlining

-Colour brush pens, sketch pens, or colour pencils (no wet media)

location.name: MAP Bengaluru, 22, Kasturba Rd, Shanthala Nagar, Ashok Nagar, Bengaluru, Karnataka, 560001, India

name: Being Queen is My Resistance: Lessons in Drag Mythology and Migration
description:
Blending storytelling, drag performance, dance, and multimedia elements, the program traces KaMani Sutra’s journey of discovering femininity while navigating life as an adult immigrant in the United States.

Rooted in the richness of Indian drag aesthetics and cultural influences, this artist's showcase reflects on identity, migration, and self-expression, contrasting the freedom of living authentically in a foreign land with the complexities of belonging and visibility. Through short performances, video, and consensual audience participation, the lecture-performance-artist showcase challenges ideas around the model minority myth, binary gender, and colonial saviorship.

At once celebratory and reflective, the program honours intergenerational queerness, LGBTQ+ community, saris, mujras, divine femininity, and the transformative power of self-love.

This programme is part of LGBTQIA+ programming and is an attempt to amplify narratives from within the queer community through film, dialogue, and collective reflection.

name: Such Treasure Revived: A Garden Walk and Visit to the Archives
description:
Join us for a walkthrough by Dhatri S at the herbal gardens at the National Centre for Biological Sciences (NCBS) campus - a serene green spot nestled between a maze of labs, and beloved by students, staff, faculty, and resident cats alike.

Its roots trace to a groundbreaking exhibit in 2008, where former cell biologist and scholar of Indian botanical-medicinal traditions, Annamma Spudich, brought 50 medicinal plants described in the Hortus Malabaricus and planted a living exhibition in its spot. This exhibition, titled "Such Treasure and Rich Merchandize: Indian Botanical knowledge in 16th and 17th Century European Books", was not only a unique collaboration between horticulturalists, artists, and historians, but also one of the many growing efforts by the institution to reflect on history and society.

Almost two decades later, the garden thrives, but how much of a curation remains? And how have those initial ideas grown? Discover its story and learn about the Archives at NCBS from Shreekant and Mahesh, and their work to document contemporary botanical records amongst its larger catalogue of history of science.

This programme is in collaboration with NCBS, and in conjunction with MAP's ongoing exhibition, Paper Gardens: Art, Botany and Empire.

name: What the Sky Sees
description:
Beneath the Turning Sky explores how we find our place in this world. How have our choices shaped what we are left with today, and what are the weighty myths, stories and headlines that trace our endless attempts to understand the cosmos?

In this workshop, participants will be given a peer-led walkthrough of Beneath the Turning Sky by Job, one of our Teen Takeover participants, following which they will be required to research and act out various time periods covered within the exhibition and depict the cycles of wonder, destruction, exploitation and creation.  

Through this exercise, the hope is to help us understand the vicious cycles that we are stuck in and how to break out of them and imagine more hopeful futures.

This programme is hosted in conjunction with the Teen Takeover programme.

name: In Search of Knowledge and Riches
description:
Image credits: Voyage to Calicut Series, Tapestry, Silk and Wool, 1504, Portuguese National Library, Lisbon
For centuries India was the nexus of a global trade in spices, medicines, dyes, unguents and perfumes—high‑value goods whose value rested on deep, native knowledge of plants and healing. In this online talk, renowned researcher Dr. Annamma Spudich traces how archaeological finds and trade records from the first millennium onwards reveal India’s sophisticated botanical and medical systems, and why by the Middle Ages “India trade was the backbone of the international economy.”

Learn how the late‑15th‑century European search for direct sea routes brought Spain, Portugal, and later the Dutch and British, into India, and how, finding their own medical knowledge inadequate for the tropics, turned to Indian physicians and botanical expertise. Dr Spudich will examine these European documents from the 16th–19th centuries that recorded Indian medicinal, agricultural and horticultural knowledge, and show how these encounters broadened Western science, inspired artists and poets and reshaped global history.

This online talk is in conjunction with MAP’s ongoing exhibition, Paper Gardens: Art, Botany and Empire, bringing together over a hundred botanical illustrations, tracing encounters between plants, artists, and institutions, and reanimating the networks of labour and imagination that have built our understanding of the natural world.

name: A Fungi Walk at Lalbagh Botanical Gardens
description:
Join us for an immersive Fungi Walk with Mamlakatoi as we explore the hidden world of mushrooms, mycelium, and forest decomposers. Led by a nature guide, you'll learn to spot fungi species, understand their vital role in our ecosystem, and discover the magic right beneath your feet.

This programme is in conjunction with MAP’s ongoing exhibition, Paper Gardens: Art, Botany and Empire.

name: Holding Space: Feminist Reading Circle
description:
What does vulnerability look like? What does care feel like?
These are the questions that artist Afra Eisma leaves us with in Warrior Garments.

Join us for a three-part series of reflective feminist reading sessions, developed in collaboration with Zubaan and  facilitated by Laxmi Murthy. Together, we will explore gender-based violence—its lived realities, and the possibilities of resistance, solidarity, and healing. Through stories, essays, and personal reflections, you are invited to engage with the exhibition at your own pace, in a space that encourages listening, sharing, and care.

Note:

- This is the second session in a series presented alongside Warrior Garments. Each session is self-contained, but we encourage participants to attend all three.

- The next session will take place on June 21, 2026.

- Reading materials for each will be shared after registration.

Trigger Warning: The sessions may include discussions on sexual violence.
